Document [0] (Title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephritic Syndrome) As with nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndromes can also be primary or secondary. Some of the most common causes of nephritic syndrome are post-infectious glomerulonephritis, IgA nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. Poststreptococcal nephritic syndrome arises 2 to 3 weeks after group A B-hemolytic nephritogenic streptococcal infection of the skin (impetigo) or pharynx. It usually occurs in children but can also occur in adults. No characteristic finding on H&E stain but granular IF pattern and subepithelial immune complex deposition (“humps”) on EM. [24] IgA is the most common nephropathy worldwide. It is characterized by the IgA immune complex deposition in the mesangium of glomeruli. It commonly presents as hematuria following mucosal infections, especially gastroenteritis. [24]

Document [3] (Title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Primary Nephrotic Syndrome) Minimal Changes are the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in children. It is idiopathic in most cases, but sometimes it is associated with neoplasias, recent infections, or vaccination. It is characterized by normal glomeruli appearance on H&E stain, negative immunofluorescence (IF) given no immune complex deposits, and effacement of foot processes on electron microscopy (EM). [25]

Document [12] (Title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Prognosis) For patients with focal-segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), the prognosis is grave. [56] Generally will progress to an end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis and kidney transplant. Only around 20% of patients with focal glomerulosclerosis go into remission of proteinuria; another 10% improve but stay proteinuric. Between 25 and 30% of patients with FSGS develop end-stage renal disease (ESRD) within five years. There have been some studies to suggest a better 5-year renal outcome in Chinese adults with primary FSGS in comparison to the west. [57] Of patients with membranous nephropathy, around 30% undergo spontaneous remission. However, for patients with persistent nephrotic syndrome, 40% to 50% develop ESRD over a period of ten years.
Document [13] (Title: Glomerulonephritis -- Prognosis -- Among the Nephrotic Spectrum Diseases) Minimal change disease has a very good prognosis for all ages if there is a response to corticosteroid therapy. The primary morbidity is related to the adverse effects of the medications. [44] Approximately a third of patients with membranous nephropathy who have subnephrotic proteinuria respond to conservative management. Spontaneous remission has also been seen in cases of heavy proteinuria. However, in others with features of nephrotic syndrome, remission may take up to 6 months, provided adequate treatment is given. Appropriate treatment does slow the progression of HIV-associated nephropathy, but with progression into ESRD, a kidney transplant may be necessary. Amyloid light-chain (AL) amyloidosis takes 2 to 3 years for progression towards ESRD, while for amyloid A (AA) amyloidosis, remission can be achieved by identifying and managing the underlying disease. Document [20] (Title: Membranous Nephropathy -- Epidemiology) Membranous nephropathy (MN) is the second most common nephropathy in adults after focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). [5] It is the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in White adults. [6] It most commonly occurs above 40 years of age, with the peak incidence between 50 to 60 years in the USA. It is more common in males than females and tends to show a better outcome in females. The incidence is 8 to 10 cases per 1 million population worldwide, and 12 per 1 million population per year in the USA. MN is less common in children and mostly due to secondary causes. [7] It can also present in conjunction with other types of glomerulonephritis like IgA nephropathy, FSGS, and lupus nephritis.

Document [26] (Title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephrotic Syndrome) Nephrotic syndrome may be primary or secondary. Document [27] (Title: Membranous Glomerulonephritis (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) MN is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes dialysis nurses. Traditionally, initial supportive care which includes controlling the blood pressure, salt restriction, ACE-I/ARB, statins, and diuretics should be provided for all patients with MN. Treatment of secondary MN should be achieved by treating the underlying cause. For patients with primary MN, clinicians should be aware that the addition of immunosuppressive (IS) therapy to the supportive care is indicated for patients with progressive loss of GFR (greater than 50% in serum creatinine or a level greater than 1.5 mg/dl) or refractory proteinuria. The Toronto risk score can be used to categorize patients in low (less than 4% g per day), moderate (4 to 8 g per day), and high risk (greater than 8 g per day) after 6 months of supportive care. Moderate and high-risk patients should be provided with IS therapy.

Document [31] (Title: Glomerulonephritis -- Treatment / Management) IgA nephropathy: ACE inhibitors/ARBs (3 to 6 months) are used as they reduce proteinuria. Corticosteroids and fish oil can be prescribed if proteinuria exceeds 1 gm (provided GFR>50) even after the initial therapy. Henoch Schonlein purpura (HSP) is managed on the same lines. Steroids are also helpful for gastrointestinal tract (GIT) related symptoms here. Post Streptococcal GN: Supportive treatment and antibiotics to get rid of nephritogenic bacteria. [40] Anti-GBM Disease: The available options include plasma exchange, corticosteroids, rituximab, and cyclophosphamide. Together, these relationships highlight how various forms of glomerular inflammation, sclerosis, and nephrotic processes intersect within drug‑induced syndromes, autoimmune disease clusters, and post‑infectious contexts, underscoring the importance of recognizing shared pathogenic pathways for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2919) GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA is identified as the English equivalent of the Arabic term التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد, which describes acute inflammation of the kidney glomeruli. This specific condition is classified within the broader parent category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating that acute glomerular inflammation is considered a type of renal disease encompassed by these related disorders. Recognizing GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA as a subset of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome helps integrate terminology across languages and situates the condition within the larger framework of kidney pathology for clinical assessment and management.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 821) Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a subtype of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and therefore falls under the broader category of kidney glomerular pathology. It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), reflecting a pathogenic link to auto‑immune injury of the glomerular basement membrane. In clinical practice, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often presents as part of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), indicating simultaneous involvement of the lungs and kidneys. The progression of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى can lead to اليوريمية (uremia), underscoring the potential for severe renal failure if the underlying glomerular injury is not promptly recognized and treated. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated management of patients with necrotizing glomerulonephritis and related systemic manifestations.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913) The CPTAC Medical Conditions Codelist includes the condition التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد (acute glomerulonephritis). This condition is classified under the broader parent category nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating that acute glomerulonephritis is a specific form of renal inflammatory disease within that group.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262) Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), and it also has a relationship with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي manifests as اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), and this renal impairment is a central feature of the pulmonary‑renal syndrome that accompanies necrotizing glomerulonephritis. Recognizing that necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑GBM disease, renal dysfunction, and pulmonary‑renal syndrome are interlinked helps clinicians diagnose and manage the combined kidney‑lung involvement more effectively.

Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 193) is identified as a severe form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease) and classified within the broader categories of nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis; it is associated with post‑infectious glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) and contributes to اليوريمية and kidney dysfunction. Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2687) Impaired is a descriptive term that can denote functional deficits in distinct clinical contexts. In the neurodevelopmental domain, Impaired is interpreted as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي), a practical social communication disorder that reflects difficulties in interacting socially. In the renal domain, Impaired is also used as an interpretation of قصور كلوي مزمن, indicating chronic kidney disease and the progressive loss of kidney function. The renal condition chronic kidney disease (قصور كلوي مزمن) is clinically associated with التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), underscoring that glomerular inflammation often contributes to or co‑exists with long‑term renal impairment. Recognizing that the label “Impaired” may refer to either social communication deficits or chronic kidney dysfunction, and that glomerulonephritis is linked to chronic kidney disease, helps clinicians accurately interpret the term in context and tailor patient assessment and management accordingly.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1361) Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is classified within the broader category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, reflecting its relationship to inflammatory and protein‑leakage kidney disorders. A specific subtype, chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, represents CKD that arises as a consequence of long‑standing high blood pressure, highlighting hypertension as an important etiologic factor for renal impairment. In clinical data collection, the entry “Unknown” is recognized as a permissible value for the CKD variable, allowing healthcare providers to record cases where the presence or cause of CKD cannot be definitively determined. Recognizing CKD’s placement under nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, its hypertension‑related form, and the allowance for an “Unknown” status supports accurate diagnosis, categorization, and documentation in patient care.

Acute glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد) is a broad clinical category that encompasses nephritis, nephrosis, nephrotic syndrome, and renal sclerosis; it is synonymous with acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) and is a member of acute renal failure (الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ)). Morphological subtypes of acute glomerulitis include crescentic glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي) and hyperplastic glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري). Hyperplastic glomerulitis is also mapped to post‑inflammatory glomerulitis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) and can manifest in systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)). Pediatric Nephrology Terminology adds متلازمة انحلال الدم الوريمي (hemolytic‑uremic syndrome) and crescentic glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي) as conditions linked to the CKD SMQ grouping; both are considered members of the chronic kidney disease framework.
